text: The surgeon applies a clamp to the renal artery, selectively occluding the vessel supplying the upper pole of the kidney to prevent blood flow. The small tumor at the upper pole is carefully dissected and peeled away, with minimal bleeding observed due to the occlusion of the feeding vessel.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis by suturing the base of the kidney using a barbed V-lock suture with a large needle. The clamp on the renal artery is removed, restoring blood flow to the kidney, with minimal bleeding observed due to the tumor's location at the kidney's edge. The suture is run up the length of the base, effectively holding the tissue without slippage.

It's the stitch that I first used during my prostatectomies for prostate cancer, but then I found it transferred very nicely to the partial nephrectomy setting. I find it quite easy to achieve hemostasis and close the kidney using this suture. I think the robot has really really helped with this operation more than any other operation. This type of suturing laparoscopically can be technically challenging and when time is of the essence and it's in a stressful situation then that adds further difficulties. The robot makes suturing so much more simple and straightforward because of the wristed movements of the instruments. It allows you to create different angles to allow you to place the needle in the best possible place. It also allows transfer of the needle from one needle holder to the other very easily. It makes for a swift closure of the kidney.

As I say, I think it is the biggest advance in surgery, the robots, for this operation. No longer need to use a, to have a large, potentially morbid open loin incision, which sometimes needed the, or led to the breaking of the rib. I think you've definitely removed some of the potential complications you saw with laparoscopic partial nephrectomy, such as bleeding, urine leaks and prolonged clamping of the kidney, leading to deteriorating renal function. This was a relatively simple, straightforward case The robot allows you to now potentially tackle more challenging tumours in terms of location within the kidney and also size. You can now potentially attempt to remove larger tumours because of the advantages of the robot.

The surgeon uses a hemlock clip to snug down the barbed suture, running it along the outside of the kidney to close the defect. The barbs on the stitch help hold the kidney tissue together, allowing for precise tension adjustment with the clip.
